text: Hi, this is Dr. Vishal Soni. I'm a consultant laparoscopic surgeon at Zydus Hospital, Ahmedabad. And today we'll be seeing a laparoscopic appendectomy in a case of acute appendicitis.

The optical trocar is visible, along with two 10 mm trocars and one 5 mm walking port. The inflamed appendix is identified, and the surgeon uses a ligature in the right hand to perform serial division of the mesoappendix, effectively devascularizing the appendix.
The surgeon identifies the appendicular artery and ensures its management. The mesoappendix is divided close to the appendix, with clear visualization of the separation process.
The surgeon performs further dissection at the base of the appendix, which appears lean due to prior devascularization, facilitating easier extraction of the specimen.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the lateral attachments of the appendix, revealing the thickened and inflamed peritoneum due to the inflammatory process. The appendicular base is clearly visible, continuing into the cecum, which is the intended site for placing endo loops.

The surgeon applies two endo loops to the base of the appendix, securing it on the patient side before cutting the appendix. The decision is made not to use a third loop due to minimal inflammation observed in the appendix.
The surgeon cauterizes the mucosa of the appendix stump and extracts the appendix specimen through the umbilical port.
The surgeon completes a thorough inspection of the abdomen and terminal ileum, ensuring no residual issues. The omentum is repositioned near the base of the appendix.
